channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: Rare Blue Micromoon to rise this weekend - MLive.com
item.description:
1. Rare Blue Micromoon to rise this weekend  MLive.com
  2. How and when to watch May’s blue moon  CNN
  3. Blue micromoon visible in Maryland this weekend  WBFF
  4. How to catch the perfect full moonrise — just in time for the Blue Moon show on May 30  Space
  5. How to Best See This Weekend’s Rare Blue Micromoon  Time Magazine

item.title: NASA's next major space telescope, the Nancy Grace Roman, is expected to find roughly 100,000 new transiting planets in just five years — along with the largest catalogue ever assembled of rogue worlds drifting through the galaxy without a star to orbit - Space Daily
item.description:
1. NASA's next major space telescope, the Nancy Grace Roman, is expected to find roughly 100,000 new transiting planets in just five years — along with the largest catalogue ever assembled of rogue worlds drifting through the galaxy without a star to orbit  Space Daily
  2. Peering into the Milky Way's far side, Roman could unveil 100,000 worlds  Phys.org
  3. Roman telescope will observe an unexplored part of the Milky Way  Universe Space Tech
  4. Moment of Science: NASA’s Roman Space Telescope set to revolutionize astronomy  WTVG
  5. NASA’s Roman Space Telescope Primary Mirror Gets Last Look  NASA (.gov)

item.title: To travel far on cloudy days, pigeons listen to their gut - CNN
item.description:
1. To travel far on cloudy days, pigeons listen to their gut  CNN
  2. How do pigeons sense Earth's magnetic field? Scientists say they’ve solved the mystery.  National Geographic
  3. A new study says homing pigeon livers act like compasses. Other experts aren’t so sure  Scientific American
  4. Pigeons may be navigating with their liver, study suggests  AP News
  5. Homing pigeons may use a surprising navigation mechanism  Science News

item.title: James Webb Space Telescope discovers a black hole that formed before its host galaxy. Scientists aren't sure how - Space
item.description:
1. James Webb Space Telescope discovers a black hole that formed before its host galaxy. Scientists aren't sure how  Space
  2. A direct black-hole mass measurement in a little red dot at high redshift  Nature
  3. Scientists Found a Black Hole That Breaks The Rules of Astrophysics  ScienceAlert
  4. Webb reveals black hole that formed before its galaxy  Phys.org
  5. Little Red Dot Abell2744-QSO1: Sonification of Gas Velocity Around a Supermassive Black Hole (NIRCam and NIRSpec IFU)  NASA Science (.gov)

item.title: NASA Uses Mineralogical Marker to Understand Ancient Martian Climate - NASA Science (.gov)
item.description:
1. NASA Uses Mineralogical Marker to Understand Ancient Martian Climate  NASA Science (.gov)

  4. Mineral ring on Mars reveals an ocean that lasted a million years  Earth.com
  5. Mineral clues in Gale Crater track ancient Mars climate change  Phys.org

item.title: Severed sea cucumber appendages don’t seem to die - Ars Technica
item.description:
1. Severed sea cucumber appendages don’t seem to die  Ars Technica
  2. A severed piece of sea cucumber refused to die, and what happened next could transform medicine  Phys.org
  3. Can Cells from a Sea Cucumber Live Forever?  Nautilus | Science
  4. Sea cucumber tissue heals itself and survives for years after being detached from the body  Earth.com
  5. A Marine Animal Defies the Limits of Immortality: A Fragment of Its Amputated Body Survives for Three Years in Seawater  La Brújula Verde

item.title: The asteroid that wiped out the dinosaurs may have struck a planet that was already in serious trouble — a new Johns Hopkins study has found evidence of a separate ecological crisis that began about 30,000 years before the impact, coincident with a high-vo - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The asteroid that wiped out the dinosaurs may have struck a planet that was already in serious trouble — a new Johns Hopkins study has found evidence of a separate ecological crisis that began about 30,000 years before the impact, coincident with a high-vo  Space Daily
  2. Fungi Bloomed Twice around End-Cretaceous Mass Extinction  Sci.News
  3. When The Dinosaur-Killing Asteroid Hit, This Life-Form Feasted On The Death  ScienceAlert
  4. Extinction: What were conditions like on Earth when the dinosaur-killing asteroid crashed into the planet?  FOX Weather
  5. Story of dinosaur-killer meteorite impact told in vivid detail  The Guardian

item.title: New evidence suggests Saturn's rings are younger than the dinosaurs — and for most of the planet's history, the Saturn you picture may never have existed - Space Daily

item.title: Researchers went into a 25m-deep tunnel in Greece – and found an unidentified creature clinging to the walls - BBC Wildlife Magazine
item.description:

  5. I Study the Universe. And Everything Scientists Know About the End of the World Could Be Wrong.  Popular Mechanics

item.title: A pair of American satellites built to catch the Soviets cheating on a nuclear test ban kept detecting unexplained flashes, and the flashes turned out to be the most powerful explosions in the universe coming from billions of light-years away - Space Daily
item.description: A pair of American satellites built to catch the Soviets cheating on a nuclear test ban kept detecting unexplained flashes, and the flashes turned out to be the most powerful explosions in the universe coming from billions of light-years away  Space Daily

item.title: In 1987 a star exploded in a nearby galaxy, and detectors deep underground caught the burst of neutrinos roughly three hours before any telescope on Earth saw the light arrive. - Space Daily
item.description: In 1987 a star exploded in a nearby galaxy, and detectors deep underground caught the burst of neutrinos roughly three hours before any telescope on Earth saw the light arrive.  Space Daily

item.title: Mount Everest is the highest point above sea level, but because the Earth bulges at the equator, the summit of a volcano in Ecuador sits farther from the centre of the planet, making it the closest piece of land on Earth to outer space. - Space Daily

item.title: In 1965, a 30-year-old cosmonaut named Alexei Leonov became the first human to walk in space — and somewhere in the middle of the live broadcast, while the world was watching the Soviet Union's greatest propaganda triumph, mission control quietly cut t - Space Daily

item.title: Earth is quietly dusted with thousands of tonnes of space material every year, most of it as grains smaller than sand — and some of it can be sifted from the grit of ordinary roof gutters, tiny meteorites hiding in plain sight in cities. - Space Daily
